items.q: What were Impressionist painters primarily trying to capture?
items.correct: The fleeting effects of light and atmosphere at a particular moment.
items.misconception: Their feelings or first impressions of a scene.
items.why: Students take 'impression' as personal feeling. Impressionism was about visual perception - how light actually appears on surfaces, painted quickly outdoors before light changed.

items.q: What are the three primary colours in paint (subtractive)?
items.correct: Red, yellow, and blue (or magenta, yellow, cyan in print).
items.why: Students confuse light (additive: RGB) with paint/print (subtractive: RYB or CMY). RGB mixes light to white; RYB/CMY mixes pigment toward black - different colour models, different primaries.

items.q: What attitude does Pop Art take toward consumer culture?
items.correct: Ambiguous - it both celebrates and critiques mass media imagery.
items.why: Students read Pop Art as either pure celebration or pure protest. Warhol's deadpan repetition of Marilyn or soup cans is deliberately AMBIGUOUS, leaving viewers to decide if it's homage or critique.

items.q: Which technique uses crossing lines for tone?
items.why: Correct answer: Cross-hatching. “Stippling” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Dots. “Lines” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many primary colours are commonly used in pigment mixing?
items.why: Exact answer: 3 colours. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
